title: Epistemic Infrastructure
subtitle: Toward systems that navigate knowledge rather than generate it
abstract: As AI systems become primary mediators of human knowledge, the question shifts from 'what can models generate?' to 'what kind of epistemic environment do they create?' We argue for a distinction between knowledge generators and knowledge navigators, and sketch what infrastructure optimized for the latter might look like.
body:
## Motivation

The dominant paradigm in applied AI treats knowledge as something to be generated — a model ingests a corpus and produces new text. But generation and navigation are not the same cognitive act. A library does not write books; it makes them findable. A map does not create terrain; it makes it traversable.

Most AI systems today are optimized for fluency and coverage. Very few are optimized for what we might call epistemic integrity — the property of helping a user understand not just what is true, but how confident to be, where the live disputes are, and what they themselves actually believe after encountering the system.

## The Missing Layer

Between raw information and human understanding lies a layer that has no good name. We call it the epistemic infrastructure — the set of structures, practices, and systems that determine how a society processes evidence, forms beliefs, and revises them in light of new data.

Print created one such layer. Libraries created another. Search engines created a third. Each changed not just what people knew, but how they knew it — what counted as a source, what counted as settled, what counted as contested.

AI systems are creating a fourth layer, but without deliberate design around its epistemic properties. The result is systems optimized for engagement over calibration, for fluency over accuracy, for confident assertion over honest uncertainty.

## What Navigator-Oriented Systems Look Like

A knowledge navigator, as we define it, has four properties:

**Calibration.** It represents its own uncertainty accurately. When a domain is contested, it says so. When evidence is thin, it flags that rather than papering over it with confident prose.

**Provenance.** It maintains legible connections between claims and their sources, so a user can inspect the chain of reasoning rather than receiving conclusions as if from an oracle.

**Divergence awareness.** It tracks where reasonable people disagree and why — not to relativize all claims, but to accurately represent the epistemic landscape.

**Exit ramps.** It actively helps users develop the capacity to evaluate claims independently, rather than fostering dependence on the system itself.

## Faith, Truth, Love

These properties are not merely technical. They correspond to deeper orientations: calibration to Truth (the commitment to represent the world as it is), provenance to something like intellectual humility (acknowledging that knowledge is received and constructed, not merely generated), and exit ramps to Love (the orientation toward the flourishing of the person you're speaking with, not just the satisfaction of the immediate query).

The NSO research agenda is organized around building systems with these properties — not as constraints on otherwise unconstrained capability, but as the core design target.

title: SEAM: Structured Epistemic Alignment Mechanism
subtitle: A framework for building calibrated belief into language model pipelines
abstract: We introduce SEAM (Structured Epistemic Alignment Mechanism), a pipeline architecture that augments language model outputs with explicit uncertainty, provenance, and divergence signals. Unlike approaches that treat calibration as a fine-tuning objective, SEAM operates at inference time through a layered reasoning scaffold that separates claim generation from claim evaluation.
body:
## Overview

Current approaches to improving model truthfulness typically target training: RLHF, constitutional AI, and related methods shape model behavior by adjusting weights. These approaches have real value but face a fundamental limitation — they bake epistemic properties into the model at training time, making them static and difficult to inspect or audit.

SEAM takes a different approach. Rather than training for calibration, it structures the inference pipeline so that calibration is a visible, inspectable artifact of the generation process.

## Architecture

The SEAM pipeline has three layers:

### Layer 1: Claim Extraction

The first layer takes a model's raw output and decomposes it into atomic claims — statements that can be individually evaluated. This is itself a learned process, but one focused narrowly on decomposition rather than generation.

### Layer 2: Epistemic Tagging

Each extracted claim passes through a tagging step that assigns:

- **Confidence level** (high / medium / low / contested)
- **Claim type** (empirical, normative, definitional, predictive)
- **Provenance class** (well-sourced, model-internal, unknown)
- **Dispute flag** (whether meaningful expert disagreement exists)

### Layer 3: Recomposition

The tagged claims are recomposed into a response that preserves the original's fluency while surfacing epistemic metadata. Depending on the interface, this metadata may be displayed inline, in tooltips, or in a parallel panel.

## Why This Matters

The practical value of SEAM is not primarily accuracy improvement — well-calibrated models are already fairly accurate on well-sourced domains. The value is legibility. A user interacting with a SEAM-augmented system can see where the model is confident and where it is guessing, where claims are well-sourced and where they depend on the model's internal priors.

This legibility is the foundation of the navigator property. A system that shows its work can be questioned, corrected, and used as a scaffold rather than an oracle.

## Current Status

SEAM is under active development. The claim extraction and tagging layers have been prototyped on several domains. We are currently evaluating recomposition approaches and working on the interface design problem — how to surface epistemic metadata in a way that informs rather than overwhelms.

title: Non-Stationarity and the Alignment Problem
subtitle: Why objectives that drift over time require a different research agenda
abstract: Standard alignment framings assume that human values, while complex and difficult to specify, are at least approximately stable over the relevant timeframe. We argue this assumption is false in the ways that matter most. Human values are non-stationary — they evolve, sometimes rapidly, in response to experience, culture, and interaction with technology itself. This has significant implications for alignment research.
body:
## The Stationarity Assumption

Most alignment research operates, implicitly or explicitly, under what we call the stationarity assumption: that the values we are trying to align AI systems to are fixed enough that we can treat them as a stable target. We identify the target values, build systems that approximate them, and evaluate success by how well the system tracks the target.

This assumption is technically convenient. Fixed targets can be specified, approximated, and evaluated. Non-stationary targets are much harder to work with.

## Why the Assumption Fails

Values are non-stationary for at least three reasons:

**Cultural evolution.** Human moral frameworks have changed dramatically over historical time, and continue to change. Practices that were widely considered acceptable in 1800 are now widely considered abhorrent. Practices that are now contested will likely be settled — in one direction or another — within decades.

**Interaction effects.** Values change in response to what is possible. The widespread availability of a technology changes not just behavior but the normative expectations around behavior. AI systems will change what is possible, and therefore what is expected, and therefore what is valued.

**Reflexivity.** AI systems that are widely deployed will themselves become part of the cultural environment that shapes values. A system designed to satisfy current values will contribute to the evolution of those values, creating a feedback loop that standard alignment framings have no way to model.

## Implications

If values are non-stationary in these ways, several things follow:

First, value specification at training time is inherently limited. Any fixed specification will drift from actual human values as those values evolve. This is not a problem that better specification can solve — it's a structural feature of deploying systems over time.

Second, the relevant question is not just 'does the system satisfy current values?' but 'what kind of value evolution does the system promote?' A system can satisfy current values while systematically nudging them in directions its designers did not intend and users would not endorse if they understood the dynamics.

Third, navigator-oriented systems have an advantage here. A system designed to support human epistemic autonomy — to help users reason well and form their own beliefs — is less likely to drive value drift than a system optimized to satisfy current preferences. The latter optimizes against a moving target in a way that can steer the target.

## The NSO Agenda

Non-Stationary Objectives takes its name from this problem. We believe the most important questions in AI alignment are not about specifying fixed value targets but about designing systems that remain well-oriented as values evolve — systems that support rather than undermine the human capacity to do the moral reasoning that navigating a changing world requires.
